title_en: Salah: Prayer Law in the Maliki School
content_en:
The Maliki school's prayer law, as expounded by ad-Dardiri in Al-Sharh al-Kabir, presents a number of distinctive positions that have shaped Muslim worship across North Africa, West Africa, and historically throughout Andalusia and parts of the Arab world.

The Maliki school identifies seventeen obligatory elements (fara'id) of prayer, a notably detailed list that includes several acts the other schools treat as recommended. These obligatory elements are: the opening takbir, the intention joined to the opening takbir, standing when able, the recitation of al-Fatiha in every rak'ah, the ruku' (bowing), the i'tidal (rising from bowing), the two sajdahs (prostrations), the julus (sitting) between the two prostrations, the final tashahhud, the final sitting for the tashahhud, the taslim to the right, and tuma'ninah (tranquility) in each position. The Maliki school also includes the opening du'a (wajib on most positions) and other elements.

A significant Maliki position concerns the recitation of al-Fatiha. Like the other three schools, the Maliki school holds that al-Fatiha must be recited in every rak'ah of prayer — but the Maliki school does not require the person praying behind an imam (muqtadi) to recite al-Fatiha in prayers that are performed silently. In silent prayers (Dhuhr and 'Asr), the follower recites al-Fatiha. In loud prayers (Fajr, Maghrib, 'Isha), the Maliki school holds that the follower is silent while the imam recites, as the imam's recitation suffices. Ad-Dardiri explains this position in Al-Sharh al-Kabir as reflecting the authentic Medinan practice.

The Maliki school does not regard the Basmala (Bismillah ir-Rahman ir-Rahim) as a verse of al-Fatiha and therefore does not include it in the obligatory recitation. It is recited quietly, if at all, before al-Fatiha — not aloud. This contrasts with the Shafi'i school, which considers the Basmala to be the first verse of al-Fatiha and recites it aloud in loud prayers.

With respect to raising the hands (raf' al-yadayn) during prayer, the Maliki school holds that raising the hands is done only at the opening takbir, not when bowing, rising from bowing, or at other points during the prayer. This is a notable difference from the Hanbali and Shafi'i schools, which follow the narrations indicating that the Prophet (peace be upon him) raised his hands at multiple points, and from the Hanafi school, which permits it at several positions. Ad-Dardiri notes this as the established Medinan practice transmitted through Malik.

The Maliki school does not hold the qunut supplication to be sunnah in Fajr prayer — aligning with the Hanafi and Hanbali positions and differing from the Shafi'i school. However, the Maliki school does prescribe qunut during the witr prayer in the last half of Ramadan, which it considers a recommended practice.

With respect to the Friday prayer (jumu'ah), the Maliki school requires twelve men for the prayer to be valid — fewer than the Shafi'i requirement of forty but more than the Hanafi minimum of three (or four, in some positions). The two khutbahs (sermons) are obligatory, and their delivery after the zenith of the sun is a requirement. Ad-Dardiri discusses the precise conditions for jumu'ah validity in Al-Sharh al-Kabir with characteristic precision.

The chapter on prayer in Al-Sharh al-Kabir is one of the most comprehensive treatments of Maliki salah law available, and its study in combination with al-Dasuqi's hashiyah gives students access to the full richness of the school's analysis, including its engagement with variant opinions within the school and with the positions of other madhabs.
title_id: Shalat: Hukum Shalat dalam Mazhab Maliki
content_id:
Hukum shalat mazhab Maliki, sebagaimana diuraikan oleh ad-Dardiri dalam Al-Syarh al-Kabir, menyajikan sejumlah posisi khas yang telah membentuk ibadah Muslim di Afrika Utara, Afrika Barat, dan secara historis di seluruh Andalusia dan bagian-bagian dunia Arab.

Mazhab Maliki mengidentifikasi tujuh belas unsur wajib (fara'idh) shalat, sebuah daftar yang cukup terperinci yang mencakup beberapa tindakan yang diperlakukan mazhab-mazhab lain sebagai dianjurkan. Unsur-unsur wajib ini mencakup: takbir pembuka, niat yang digabungkan dengan takbir pembuka, berdiri jika mampu, membaca al-Fatihah di setiap rakaat, ruku', i'tidal, dua sujud, duduk di antara dua sujud, tasyahud akhir, duduk untuk tasyahud akhir, salam ke kanan, dan tuma'ninah dalam setiap posisi.

Posisi Maliki yang signifikan berkaitan dengan bacaan al-Fatihah. Mazhab Maliki tidak mewajibkan orang yang shalat di belakang imam (makmum) untuk membaca al-Fatihah dalam shalat yang dibaca keras. Dalam shalat yang keras (Subuh, Maghrib, Isya), mazhab Maliki berpendapat bahwa makmum diam sementara imam membaca, karena bacaan imam sudah mencukupi. Ad-Dardiri menjelaskan posisi ini dalam Al-Syarh al-Kabir sebagai mencerminkan praktik Madinah yang otentik.

Mazhab Maliki tidak menganggap Basmala sebagai ayat al-Fatihah dan karena itu tidak memasukkannya dalam bacaan wajib. Basmala dibaca pelan, jika sama sekali, sebelum al-Fatihah — bukan dengan keras. Ini berbeda dengan mazhab Syafi'i yang menganggap Basmala sebagai ayat pertama al-Fatihah dan membacanya keras dalam shalat-shalat yang dibaca keras.

Berkenaan dengan mengangkat tangan (raf' al-yadayn) dalam shalat, mazhab Maliki berpendapat bahwa mengangkat tangan hanya dilakukan saat takbir pembuka, bukan saat ruku', bangkit dari ruku', atau pada titik-titik lain selama shalat. Ini berbeda secara mencolok dari mazhab Hanbali dan Syafi'i. Ad-Dardiri mencatat ini sebagai praktik Madinah yang telah mapan yang ditransmisikan melalui Imam Malik.

Bab tentang shalat dalam Al-Syarh al-Kabir adalah salah satu perlakuan paling komprehensif tentang hukum shalat Maliki yang tersedia, dan studinya dikombinasikan dengan hasyiyah al-Dasuqi memberi mahasiswa akses ke kekayaan penuh analisis mazhab, termasuk keterlibatannya dengan pendapat-pendapat yang bervariasi dalam mazhab dan posisi-posisi mazhab-mazhab lain.
